full_response:
Solution to part (c)

We wish to understand qualitatively why the two “radial–angular’’ line elements

  (1)  Euclidean plane:   ds² = dr² + r² dφ²  
  (2)  Lobachevsky (hyperbolic) plane:   ds² = du² + cosh²(u) dv²  

define geometries of very different character.

Assumptions:
  • Both are 2-dimensional Riemannian metrics with coordinates (r,φ) or (u,v).  
  • φ and v are angular coordinates, each ranging 0 to 2π.  
  • We compare intrinsic properties (curvature, circle circumferences, parallel‐line behavior).

1.  Circle circumferences  
    — In Euclidean (1): A “circle’’ of radius R is given by r=R, φ∈[0,2π].  
      C_E = ∫₀²π √(r² dφ²) = ∫₀²π R dφ = 2πR.  
    — In hyperbolic (2): A “circle’’ of hyperbolic radius U is u=U, v∈[0,2π].  
      C_H = ∫₀²π √(cosh²(U) dv²) = 2π cosh(U).  
    For large radius, cosh(U) ∼ ½ e^U grows exponentially, whereas 2πR grows only linearly.

2.  Gaussian curvature  
    For a metric of the form ds² = du² + f(u)² dv², the Gaussian curvature is  
      K = − (1 / f(u)) d²f/du².  
    – Euclidean: f(r)=r ⇒ d²f/dr²=0 ⇒ K_E = 0 (flat geometry).  
    – Lobachevsky: f(u)=cosh(u) ⇒ d²f/du²=cosh(u) ⇒  
         K_H = −(1 / cosh(u))·cosh(u) = −1  (constant negative curvature).  

3.  Geometric consequences  
   – In flat space parallel lines never meet and the angle sum of a triangle is π.  
   – In hyperbolic space parallel lines diverge, triangles have angle sum < π, area grows exponentially with “radius.’’

Conclusion
  The extra factor cosh²(u) in place of r² encodes a constant negative curvature (K = −1) geometry.  That is why circumferences grow exponentially, triangles behave hyperbolically, and the Lobachevsky plane is not “flat’’ like the ordinary Euclidean plane.

Final Answer boxed:

\boxed{
The metric ds²=du²+cosh²(u)\,dv² describes a 2-D space of constant negative Gaussian curvature K=−1 (hyperbolic geometry), whereas ds²=dr²+r²\,dφ² has K=0 (flat Euclidean geometry).  The cosh(u) factor causes circle circumferences to grow like 2π cosh(u) (exponentially) rather than linearly 2πr, and it produces all the familiar hyperbolic‐geometry effects.
}

Confidence: 94%

The cosh(u) factor causes circle circumferences to grow like 2π cosh(u) (exponentially) rather than linearly 2πr, and it produces all the familiar hyperbolic‐geometry effects.
